counties.cherokee-county: Cherokee County sits on the fast-growing northern edge of metro Atlanta, and Elite Catastrophe is not a daily fixture on these streets. What brings us here is weather. When a North Georgia storm line rolls across the county and leaves trees on roofs, across driveways, and down on power lines, our crew loads up in Rome and deploys to handle the emergency. That is the honest arrangement: a Rome-based, family-owned storm crew that answers the call 24/7 and drives to where the damage is. Cherokee carries a heavy tree load, from the mature oaks over older downtown neighborhoods to the leftover pines in the newer subdivisions, all rooted in red clay that softens after a long rain. When straight-line winds and hail get serious, those trees fail into houses. We work structures first: the tree through the bedroom, the limb pinning a car, the trunk blocking the only way out. Removals, trimming, and stump grinding are available too, but the reason to keep our number is the storm.
counties.paulding-county: Paulding County has filled in fast on the western side of metro Atlanta, trading open ground for wooded subdivisions where pines and hardwoods now stand close to houses. Elite Catastrophe is not local to Paulding, and we will not pretend otherwise. We are a Rome-based, family-owned storm-response crew, and what puts us on the road down here is severe weather. When a spring or summer storm saturates the red clay, drives straight-line winds through, and drops trees on homes, driveways, and power lines, we deploy from Rome and answer the emergency 24/7. The county's red clay is the quiet culprit. It holds water long after the rain stops, and a loblolly pine rooted in soft ground has little to hold when the wind leans on it. Big hardwoods come apart too, splitting or shedding heavy limbs onto whatever sits below. We run structures first every time: trees on houses, limbs over doorways, trunks across the only road out. Removals, trimming, and stump grinding follow once the immediate hazards are down.

canton.narrative: The pattern is familiar across North Georgia. A hot afternoon, a wall of storms building to the west, and then a violent stretch of wind and hail that leaves Canton homeowners staring at a tree where a tree should not be. As the seat of Cherokee County, sitting on the Etowah River amid wooded hills, Canton mixes a historic downtown with rings of newer subdivisions, and both carry serious tree load. The older streets hold mature oaks that have spread over rooftops for decades. The newer neighborhoods were carved out of pine stands, leaving tall loblollies rooted in red clay that turns soft after a long rain. When the weather hits, those are the trees that fail: pines that snap or uproot, big hardwoods that drop limbs or split, and leaners that end up leaning on a house. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned company based in Rome, and this is the honest part: we are not a Canton company, and we are not on these streets every day. What we are is storm response. When severe weather crosses Cherokee County, we deploy to Canton and work structures first, meaning the trees that have landed on homes, cars, and roads come before anything cosmetic. Emergency crews answer 24/7, because a tree through the roof at two in the morning cannot wait for business hours. Once the danger is handled, the rest of the work is available as well: removing the trees the storm damaged, trimming limbs back off the roofline, and grinding stumps so the yard is usable again. But in Canton, the call that matters most is the one right after the sky turns green.
canton.treeAngle: county seat canopy over Etowah River subdivisions

woodstock.narrative: Woodstock grew from a small railroad town into one of the largest suburbs in Cherokee County, and the older sections kept their trees the whole way. Heavy hardwood canopy shades the established neighborhoods, while the fast-growing subdivisions around them hold the tall pines that were left behind when construction took away the trees around them. It is a lot of wood hanging over a lot of rooftops, and North Georgia storms know how to find it. When straight-line winds and hail move through, the failures come in a hurry: a loblolly uprooted out of saturated clay and laid across a driveway, an oak limb punched through a garage, a whole tree leaned over onto the power line. That is when Elite Catastrophe gets the call. We are based in Rome, not Woodstock, and we will not pretend otherwise. We are the crew that deploys after the storm, not the one you see around town on a normal Tuesday. Storm response is what we do here, and we do it structures first. When a system rakes across Woodstock, our priority is the trees that have hit homes and vehicles or blocked roads, handled in the order that gets people safe and moving again. Those emergency calls are answered 24/7, through the night and through the weekend, because that is when the wind does its damage. After the emergency passes, we can take care of the rest: removing the trees the storm cracked or killed, trimming the limbs left hanging over the roof, and grinding out stumps once a removal is done. In Woodstock, though, the honest reason to keep our number is simple. When the next line of storms comes through the canopy, you will want a crew already rolling.
woodstock.treeAngle: heavy leftover canopy over fast-growing wooded subdivisions

holly-springs.narrative: Follow I-575 north through Cherokee County and Holly Springs is the growing town on either side of it, filling in with wooded neighborhoods where pines and hardwoods stand tight against new homes. That growth is why storm season matters so much here. Every subdivision carved into the woods leaves tall trees behind, and tall trees in saturated red clay are exactly what North Georgia's straight-line winds go after. The storm math in Holly Springs is unforgiving. A loblolly pine that grew up sheltered inside a stand gets left tall and exposed once construction takes away the trees around it, and after a week of rain its roots lose their grip in soft clay. One hard gust and it is across the driveway, on the roof, or hung up on the service line. Big oaks fail differently, shedding heavy limbs or splitting at weak unions, but the result on the ground looks the same. Elite Catastrophe answers those calls from our base in Rome. We are honest about the arrangement: we are not a Holly Springs outfit, and we are not on these streets day to day. We are storm response, and when severe weather crosses this stretch of Cherokee County, we deploy. Our crews work structures first, clearing the trees that have come down on houses, cars, and roads before anything else, with emergency service running 24/7. When the immediate hazard is gone, the follow-up work is there when you want it: taking down the trees the storm damaged beyond saving, trimming what is hanging over the house, and grinding the stumps flush. But along the I-575 corridor, the call that counts is the one you make with a tree already on the roof.
holly-springs.treeAngle: I-575 corridor pines over growing wooded neighborhoods

ball-ground.narrative: Up in the north end of Cherokee County, where the land starts to climb toward the mountains, Ball Ground keeps the feel of a small historic town surrounded by woods. The trees here are a mix of tall pines and mature hardwoods, many of them on wooded acreage and ridge-edge lots where the wind has a clear run at them. That exposure changes how storms play out. When a North Georgia system comes through with straight-line winds and hail, a tree standing out on a slope or an open ridge shoulder catches far more force than one tucked into town. Saturated red clay does the rest, letting a loblolly's roots slip until the whole tree goes over. What lands is the emergency: a pine dropped across the driveway, a hardwood limb through the roof, a trunk leaned onto the power line with the lights out behind it. That is the work Elite Catastrophe brings to Ball Ground. We are based in Rome, roughly forty-seven miles off, and we are straight about it: we are not a local Ball Ground crew, we are the one that deploys when the weather turns. Storm response is the whole point. When severe weather hits north Cherokee, we run structures first, clearing trees off homes, vehicles, and roads ahead of anything else, with emergency calls answered 24/7. Once the hazards are down, we can circle back to the ordinary work: removing what the storm ruined, trimming limbs off the structure, and grinding stumps so the ground is clear again. In a place as wooded and wind-exposed as Ball Ground, though, the storm call is the one that earns its keep.
ball-ground.treeAngle: north Cherokee hardwoods and pines near the mountains

waleska.narrative: Waleska is a small, rural town in Cherokee County, best known as the home of Reinhardt University and wrapped in the kind of wooded, open acreage that gives big trees plenty of room to grow. That same room means a lot of tall timber standing close to homes, barns, and drives, and when North Georgia storms arrive, all that wood becomes the problem. The failures follow the regional script. Loblolly pines, shallow-rooted in red clay that softens after days of rain, snap off or heave out of the ground when straight-line winds hit. Old oaks and other hardwoods shed heavy limbs or come apart at the trunk. On rural lots the fallen tree often takes a fence, a shed, or a stretch of driveway with it, and sometimes it is the power line that goes down under the weight. Elite Catastrophe covers Waleska as storm response out of Rome. We want to be clear about that: we are not a Waleska company, and we are not around every day. We are the crew that deploys after severe weather, and structures first is how we work, clearing trees off houses, vehicles, and roads before we touch anything else. Emergency calls are answered 24/7, because storms in this part of Cherokee County do not keep office hours. After the urgent work is done, the rest is available on your schedule: removing storm-damaged and dead trees, trimming limbs back off rooflines and outbuildings, and grinding stumps so the property is usable again. But in a rural, tree-heavy town like Waleska, the honest headline is storm response, and that is the call worth keeping our name ready for.
waleska.treeAngle: rural wooded acreage around Reinhardt University

nelson.narrative: Nelson sits right on the Cherokee and Pickens county line, a small town in old marble country where the surroundings are rural and heavily wooded. It is a quiet place most of the year. Then North Georgia's storm season arrives, and the quiet gets interrupted by the sound of trees coming down. The setup around Nelson is all tall timber and open ground. Loblolly pines stand in red clay that turns soft after a long soaking rain, and when straight-line winds and hail push through, those pines snap or lift out at the roots. Mature hardwoods fail too, dropping big limbs or splitting where the trunk forks. On these rural properties a falling tree tends to find something: a roofline, a vehicle, a driveway, or the power line running out to the house. When that happens, Elite Catastrophe deploys to Nelson from our base in Rome, a drive of about forty-eight miles. Here is the honest version: we are not a Nelson outfit, and we are not on these back roads day to day. We are storm response, plain and simple. When severe weather crosses this corner of Cherokee and Pickens, our crews run structures first, meaning the trees that have landed on homes, cars, and roads come off ahead of anything else, and emergency calls are answered 24/7. Once the immediate danger is cleared, we can take care of the rest when it suits you: removing the trees the storm wrecked, trimming what is left hanging over the house, and grinding out stumps so the yard goes back to normal. In a rural, wooded town like Nelson, though, the reason to have our number is the weather and the mess it leaves behind.
nelson.treeAngle: rural wooded lots in old marble country

dallas.narrative: Ask anyone in Dallas who has been through a rough Paulding County storm season and they can describe the aftermath: driveways blocked, limbs down across the yard, and at least one tree leaning somewhere new. As the Paulding County seat, Dallas has grown quickly, its wooded subdivisions spreading across the red clay hills that define this part of Georgia. All that new construction left tall trees standing close to new houses, and that is a setup storms know how to exploit. The clay is the quiet villain. It holds water long after the rain stops, and a loblolly pine rooted in saturated ground has very little to hang onto when straight-line winds arrive. Over it goes, across a driveway or onto a roof. The big hardwoods fail on their own terms, shedding heavy limbs without warning or splitting apart in a strong gust, and either way something on the property usually pays for it. Elite Catastrophe handles Dallas as storm response from our base in Rome, and at roughly thirty miles it is one of the closer towns on our storm map. We will be straight with you: we are not a local Dallas company, and we are not working these streets every day. When severe weather crosses Paulding County, we deploy, and we work structures first, clearing the trees that have landed on homes, vehicles, and roads before anything else. Emergency calls are answered 24/7. When the urgent part is finished, the ordinary work is available too: removing the trees the storm damaged, trimming limbs back off the house, and grinding stumps so the yard is whole again. But in a fast-growing county seat like Dallas, the honest reason to keep our number is the storm and what it leaves in the yard.
dallas.treeAngle: Paulding seat subdivisions rooted in red clay

hiram.narrative: Hiram grew up along US-278 as a retail hub for Paulding County, and the neighborhoods spreading out from that busy corridor are full of the pines and hardwoods that come standard in North Georgia. Those trees are pleasant to live under right up until a storm turns them into a hazard, and around Hiram the storms come every season. The physics are always the same. A stretch of heavy rain saturates the red clay, straight-line winds and hail arrive on a hot afternoon, and the tall loblolly pines that were left standing when the subdivisions went in start to give. Some snap partway up. Others lift out at the roots and fall whole, onto a roof, across a driveway, or down onto the power line. The oaks and other hardwoods add their own damage, dropping limbs heavy enough to crush whatever is beneath them. Elite Catastrophe answers Hiram as storm response, dispatched from our base in Rome about thirty-six miles away. We are honest about the setup: we are not a Hiram company, and we are not a daily presence here. We are the crew that deploys when the weather turns dangerous, and we work structures first, meaning the trees that have hit houses, vehicles, and roads get cleared first. Emergency calls are answered 24/7, whenever the storms decide to move. Once the danger is off the house and out of the road, we can handle the follow-up on your timeline: removing storm-damaged and dead trees, trimming the limbs left hanging over the roof, and grinding stumps so the yard is usable again. Along the US-278 corridor, though, the call that matters is the one you make with a tree already down.
hiram.treeAngle: US-278 hub pines and hardwoods over homes
